question: What's the corporate tax rate in the UAE?
answer: The corporate tax rate in the UAE is 9% on the portion of the Taxable Income exceeding AED 375,000. Source: page 21 of CTGGCT1.

question: When did corporate tax actually start in the UAE?
answer: Corporate Tax in the UAE started for Tax Periods commencing on or after 1 June 2023. Source: page 20 of CTGGCT1.

2. Introduction 
 
 
Overview 
 
Federal Decree-Law No. 47 of 2022 on the Taxation of Corporations and Businesses 
(“Corporate Tax Law”) was signed on 3 October 2022 and was published in Issue #737 
of the Official Gazette of the United Arab Emirates (“UAE”) on 10 October 2022.  
 
The Corporate Tax Law provides the legislative basis for imposing a federal tax on 
corporations and Business profits (“Corporate Tax”) in the UAE. 
 
The provisions of the Corporate Tax Law shall apply to Tax Periods commencing on 
or after 1 June 2023.

This guide is designed to provide general guidance on Corporate Tax in the UAE with 
a view to making the provisions of the Corporate Tax Law as understandable as 
possible to readers. It provides readers with: 
 
• an overview of the main Corporate Tax rules and procedures, including the 
determination of the Corporate Tax base, the calculation of Corporate Tax, the 
filing of Corporate Tax Returns, and other related compliance requirements; 
and

question: How much profit can I make before I owe any corporate tax?
answer:
You can make a profit of up to AED 375,000 before owing any corporate tax, as this portion of the taxable income is subject to a 0% tax rate. Any profit exceeding AED 375,000 will be subject to corporate tax at the applicable rate. 

Source: page 21 of CTGGCT1.

question: After my financial year ends, how long do I have to file my return?
answer:
You have to file your Tax Return and pay Corporate Tax within nine months from the end of the relevant Tax Period. For example, if your Financial Year ends on 31 December, you are required to file your Tax Return and pay Corporate Tax on or before 30 September the following year. 

Source: page 120 of CTGGCT1.

question: My free-zone company made AED 2 million profit — do I pay 0%?
answer:
To determine if your free-zone company can benefit from the 0% Corporate Tax rate, it is essential to know whether the profit is considered Qualifying Income. If the entire AED 2 million profit is classified as Qualifying Income, then you would pay 0%. However, if any portion of the profit is not Qualifying Income, that portion will be subject to the general tax rate of 9%. 

Since the context does not specify the nature of your profit, you should consult the relevant Cabinet and Ministerial Decisions regarding Qualifying Income and activities to confirm your eligibility. 

Source: page 112 of CTGGCT1.

question: My business is tiny with very low revenue. Is there relief so I pay nothing?
answer:
Yes, if your business qualifies as a small business and your Revenue does not exceed the small business relief threshold of AED 3,000,000 per Tax Period, you may benefit from small business relief. This means you would not need to calculate your Taxable Income or complete a full Tax Return, and you would be treated as having no Taxable Income for that Tax Period. However, you must make an election in your Tax Return to benefit from this relief. Additionally, you must meet compliance obligations, including registering for Corporate Tax purposes and filing a Tax Return. 

Source: page 89 of CTGGCT1.

question: My profit is below the threshold — do I still have to register at all?
answer:
If you are a Taxable Person, you are required to register for Corporate Tax with the FTA and obtain a Tax Registration Number, regardless of your profit level. However, if you are a Non-Resident Person without a Permanent Establishment in the UAE and only earn State Sourced Income, you can choose not to register. If you have a nexus in the UAE, such as earning income from Immovable Property, you are required to register. 

Source: page 116 of CTGGCT1.

question: I personally earn rent from a flat I own. Is that taxed?
answer:
Income from Personal Investments, such as rent earned from a flat owned by a natural person, is not subject to Corporate Tax. This is because such income is considered to be derived in a personal capacity and not through a business or commercial activity. 

Source: page 28 of CTGGCT1.

question: As a free-zone company, what exactly counts as 'qualifying income' for the 0%?
answer:
Qualifying Income is the income that can benefit from the 0% Corporate Tax rate for Qualifying Free Zone Persons. It includes income earned from activities that meet the criteria set out in Cabinet Decision No. 55 of 2023 and the Ministerial Decision No. 139 of 2023. However, it is important to note that Qualifying Free Zone Persons are not entitled to the 0% rate on their first AED 375,000 of Taxable Income that is not considered as Qualifying Income. Therefore, any Taxable Income that does not qualify will be subject to the standard Corporate Tax rate of 9%. 

To summarize, for income to be classified as Qualifying Income, it must be derived from activities that align with the definitions and requirements specified in the relevant decisions. 

Source: page 33 of CTGGCT1.

question: Can my group of companies be taxed as one, and what must we meet?
answer:
Yes, your group of companies can be taxed as one by forming a Tax Group, but you must meet certain conditions. The Parent Company must apply to the FTA, and both the Parent Company and its Subsidiaries must be resident juridical persons under the Corporate Tax Law and under a relevant Double Taxation Agreement (if applicable). They must also have the same Financial Year and prepare their Financial Statements using the same accounting standards. Additionally, no member of the Tax Group can be an Exempt Person or Qualifying Free Zone Person. Ownership requirements include that the Parent Company must directly or indirectly own at least 95% of the share capital, hold at least 95% of the voting rights, and be entitled to at least 95% of the Subsidiary’s profits and net assets. 

Source: page 104 of CTGGCT1.

0% rate to the first AED 375,000 of its collective Taxable Income. 
 
For further details on the Taxable Income threshold, readers are advised to consult 
Cabinet Decision No. 116 of 2022 on the Taxable Income Threshold.

8.3.1. 
Requirements to form a Tax Group 
 
In order to form a Tax Group, a Parent Company must make an application to the FTA. 
The application must be made by the Parent Company and each of the Subsidiaries 
seeking to become members of the Tax Group. Both the Parent Company and its 
Subsidiaries must be resident juridical persons under the Corporate Tax Law and

question: My business made a loss last year — can I use it to cut this year's tax?
answer:
Yes, you can use the loss from last year to reduce this year's taxable income, provided you meet certain conditions. Specifically, you must carry forward the tax loss and can offset it against your taxable income in subsequent tax periods. However, you can only use up to 75% of your taxable income for that period to offset the carried forward tax losses. Additionally, you cannot claim tax loss relief for the tax period in which you elected to benefit from small business relief. 

Source: page 79 of CTGGCT1.

question: What records must I keep, and for how long, to stay compliant?
answer:
To stay compliant, Taxable Persons and Exempt Persons must keep records and documents for seven years following the end of the Tax Period to which they relate. 

For Taxable Persons, the records typically include:
- A record of transactions in the Tax Period
- A record of assets, including details of purchases or disposals
- Order records and delivery notes
- Other relevant business correspondence

Exempt Persons must maintain records that enable their exempt status to be readily ascertained by the FTA, and the documentation required will depend on the reason for their exemption.

It is important to note that the seven-year period starts from the end of the Tax Period to which the documents relate, not from when they were created. 

Source: page 123 of CTGGCT1.

question: I freelance from home in Dubai with no trade licence — do I owe corporate tax?
answer:
As a natural person freelancing from home in Dubai, you would only be subject to Corporate Tax on the Taxable Income derived from your business activity in the UAE. However, if you do not have a trade license, it may affect your ability to be recognized as a Taxable Person. If your income is not considered taxable or if you do not meet the criteria for registration, you may not owe Corporate Tax. It is advisable to consult with the FTA or a tax professional to clarify your specific situation. 

Source: page 49 of CTGGCT1.

question: I run a free-zone online store selling to mainland UAE customers — how am I taxed?
answer:
To determine how you are taxed as a Free Zone Person running an online store selling to mainland UAE customers, you need to consider the following conditions:

1. **Qualifying Free Zone Person**: You must derive Qualifying Income from relevant transactions, maintain adequate substance in the UAE, satisfy the de minimis requirement, and not have elected to be subject to Corporate Tax under the general rates.

2. **Revenue Threshold**: If your total Revenue exceeds a certain threshold, you may be subject to different tax obligations, including the requirement to prepare and maintain audited Financial Statements.

3. **Tax Return Submission**: You are required to submit a Tax Return to the FTA within a specified timeframe after the end of each Tax Period.

If you meet the criteria for being a Qualifying Free Zone Person and have not elected to be subject to Corporate Tax, you may benefit from certain exemptions. However, if you do not meet these conditions, you may be subject to the Corporate Tax regime.

Source: page 33 of CTGGCT1.

question: We're a 3-person startup with no profit yet. Any corporate tax obligations?
answer:
As a startup with no profit, you may be eligible for small business relief if you meet the relevant requirements. This means you would not need to pay Corporate Tax, but you are still required to meet compliance obligations under the Corporate Tax Law for each Tax Period. These obligations include registering for Corporate Tax purposes, filing a Tax Return, and retaining all relevant documents and records to support your Corporate Tax filings. 

If your Revenue is equal to or below AED 3,000,000 in the relevant Tax Period and all previous Tax Periods (ending on or before 31 December 2026), you can elect to be treated as having no Taxable Income in that period, which would relieve you from the obligation to calculate your Taxable Income or complete a full Tax Return.

Source: page 88 of CTGGCT1.

question: My mainland trading company made AED 500,000 profit — roughly what do I owe?
answer:
To determine what you owe, you need to consider that the corporate tax rate is 9% on the portion of the Taxable Income exceeding AED 375,000. Since your profit is AED 500,000, you would first subtract AED 375,000 from this amount, which leaves you with AED 125,000 that is subject to the 9% tax rate. 

Therefore, your tax liability will be calculated based on this excess amount. 

Source: page [21] of CTGGCT1.

2. Introduction 
 
 
Overview 
 
Federal Decree-Law No. 47 of 2022 on the Taxation of Corporations and Businesses 
(“Corporate Tax Law”) was signed on 3 October 2022 and was published in Issue #737 
of the Official Gazette of the United Arab Emirates (“UAE”) on 10 October 2022.  
 
The Corporate Tax Law provides the legislative basis for imposing a federal tax on 
corporations and Business profits (“Corporate Tax”) in the UAE. 
 
The provisions of the Corporate Tax Law shall apply to Tax Periods commencing on 
or after 1 June 2023.

question: Exactly what penalty in AED will I get for filing 47 days late?
answer:
For filing a Tax Return late, the penalty is AED 500 for each month of delay, or part thereof, for the first twelve months. Since 47 days is more than one month but less than two months, you would incur a penalty of AED 500 for the first month of delay. 

Source: page 120 of CTGGCT1.

10.7.1. 
Exempt Persons 
 
Exempt Persons must maintain records that enable the Exempt Person’s status to be 
readily ascertained by the FTA.411 The documentation required will depend on the 
reason that a Person is exempt from Corporate Tax. 
 
10.7.2. 
How long should records be kept?
